nameMeanings.name: Conyers
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Yorkshire, of Norman origin): habitational name from either of two places in northern France, Coignières in Seine-et-Oise or Cogners in Sarthe. Alternatively, a variant of Conyer , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Conyer
meanings: English: occupational name for a minter, a coiner of money, from an agent derivative of Old French coignier ‘to stamp money, to mint’.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
Chinese:
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: and
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of French Canadian Hénault or directly of French Hunault (see Henault 2).
Catalan (Añó): Castilianized form of Anyó, probably from the old personal name Annone.
Japanese: variant of   Anno  .

variants.name: Anno
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Japanese Masayuki, Tadashi, Yoshito.
Japanese: written 安野 ‘peaceful plain’. This surname is rare in Japan proper, and may have originated in the Ryūkyū Islands, where it is pronounced   Ano  .

variants.name: Henault
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Andre, Emile, Lucien, Martial, Pierre, Camille, Fernand, Laurier, Monique, Normand, Philippe.
French (Hénault): from the ancient Germanic personal name Haginwald, composed of the elements hagin ‘enclosure, protected place’ and wald ‘power, authority’.
Altered form (Hénault) of French Hunault: from the ancient Germanic personal name Hunwald, composed of the elements hun ‘bear cub’ and wald ‘power, authority’. Compare Ano and Eno .

nameMeanings.name: Hornby
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern): habitational name from any of several places so called, including Hornby near Tunstall (North Yorkshire), Hornby in Great Smeaton (North Yorkshire), Hornby (Lancashire), or Hornby Hall in Brougham (Westmorland). The North Yorkshire placenames probably derive from the Old Norse personal name Horni + Old Norse bȳ ‘farmstead, village’, while the Lancashire and Westmorland placenames probably derive from Old Norse horn ‘horn-shaped piece of land, river bend’ + bȳ.

nameMeanings.name: Castle
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: topographic name from Anglo-Norman French, Middle English castel ‘castle, fortified building or set of buildings’, especially the residence of a feudal lord (from Late Latin castellum, a diminutive of castrum ‘fort, Roman walled city’). The name would also have denoted a servant who lived and worked at such a place, or someone who had an obligation to provide services or rent to a castle.

variants.name: Castell
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Roberto, Amarilys, Carlos, Eduardo, Estevan, Francisco, Guadalupe, Humberto, Jorge, Manuel, Mateo, Pablo.
English and Scottish: variant of   Castle  .

Manx: shortened form of Gaelic Mac Asgaill ‘son of Ásketill’, an Old Norse personal name composed of the elements áss ‘god’ + ketill ‘sacrificial cauldron, helmet’. The Manx surname is cognate with Scottish Gaelic McAskill .

variants.name: Cassell
meanings: English and Irish: habitational name of Norman origin, from Cassel in the Nord department, Flanders, France.
English: sometimes a variant of   Castle  .
Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Ó Caisile, which is probably in origin a variant of Ó Caiside, see Cassidy .

variants.name: Castel
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Benoit, Herve, Jean Michel, Thierry, Yves.  Spanish Jose, Blanco, Carlos, Rafael.
English: variant of   Castle  .
Aragonese, Catalan, and Jewish (Sephardic): variant of   Castell   3, a cognate of 5 below.

variants.name: Castles
meanings: English: variant of Castle , with post-medieval excrescent -s.
Irish (Armagh and Meath): in Ireland, this may be the English name, but it may also be an Anglicized form of Ó Caisil; see Cashell .

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: As a boy's name this represents a revival of Middle English Jan, a by‐form of John , or an adoption of the common European form with this spelling. As a girl's name it is a short form of names such as Janet and Janice .

nameMeanings.name: II
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: 1  ♂ ‘Wondrous’ or ‘enlightenment’.
2  ♀ ‘Fine jade’.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Knight John II Conyers of Sockburne and Hornby Castle was born in 1213, in Durham, England, United Kingdom, his father, Sir Knight John I Conyers, was 23 and his mother, Alicia de Yealand, was 23. He had at least 3 sons and 1 daughter with Mrs. John Conyers. He died in Hornby Castle, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Knight John II Conyers of Sockburne and Hornby Castle est né en 1213, à Durham, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Sir Knight John I Conyers, avait 23 ans et sa mère, Alicia de Yealand, avait 23 ans. Il a eu au moins 3 fils et 1 fille avec Mrs. John Conyers. Il est décédé à Hornby Castle, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Knight John II Conyers of Sockburne and Hornby Castleが1213年にイギリスイギリスダルハムで生まれた時，父Sir Knight John I Conyersは23歳，母Alicia de Yealandは23歳であった。彼はMrs. John Conyersとの間に少なくとも3人の息子と1人の娘をもうけた。彼はイギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアホールンビー カストルで死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Knight John II Conyers of Sockburne and Hornby Castle(이)가 1213년 영국 잉글랜드 뒤람에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Knight John I Conyers(은)는 23세였고, 그의 어머니 Alicia de Yealand(은)는 23세였다. 그는 Mrs. John Conyers(와)과 적어도 세 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 호른비 카스틀에서 사망했다.
